title: The Top 10 (my favorite) Horror Books of All Time (IMO)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UVdc7kYPJEs

Daniel (@dungeondive) counts down his most beloved horror literature, exploring ten books that define the genre across different eras and styles. Starting with Theodore Sturgeon's Some of Your Blood—a complex, deeply emotional vampire story told through letters and psychiatric records—Daniel highlights Sturgeon's humanist approach to dark material. Thomas Ligotti's Teatro Grotesco brings corporate workplace horror to unsettling life, while Michael Cisco's The Divinity Student offers beautiful, surreal nightmare imagery of a scholar stuffed with pages and sent to collect unknown words.

The list progresses through Robert W. Chambers' foundational The King in Yellow to Norman Partridge's perfect Halloween novel Dark Harvest, Ricky D Cornet's provocative microfictions in The Complete Butcher's Tales, and an essential anthology, To Sleep Per Chance to Dream. Daniel places Stephen King's Pet Cemetery and William Peter Blatty's The Exorcist—both masterworks of thematic depth—in the upper tiers. But his number one pick is Ray Bradbury's The October Country, a collection overflowing with exquisitely written tales that capture October's creepy, magical essence better than any other work.

----

Which of these horror classics resonate most with your personal taste—the experimental weirdness of Ligotti and Cisco, the historical gravitas of Chambers and Blatty, or Bradbury's autumnal poetry?
